questions.stem: What distinguishes software change from green-field development?

options.text: Software change never involves writing any new code
Software change excludes any form of testing
Software change always operates on existing code
Software change is performed only by the original authors

questions.explanation: Change always operates on existing code, the central premise of maintenance; green-field starts from nothing. It does write new code (it adds functionality), can be done by any maintainer, and includes verification. (SoftwareChange p.2, p.5)

questions.stem: Why does the dominance of perfective maintenance matter for the design of a change process?

options.text: It shows corrective maintenance can be ignored entirely
It motivates a process tuned for adding functionality to existing code rather than just patching bugs
It proves that most code never needs to be tested
It means refactoring should never be performed

questions.explanation: Because perfective work dominates, the canonical process is tuned for adding functionality to existing code, not merely patching. Testing is still required, corrective work still happens (~21%), and refactoring is part of pre/postfactoring. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: How does Verification fit into the phased change model?

options.text: It runs vertically alongside Prefactoring through Conclusion
It runs only during Initiation
It replaces the Postfactoring phase
It is a single final phase after Conclusion

questions.explanation: Verification runs vertically alongside the implementation phases (Prefactoring through Conclusion), guaranteeing correctness throughout. It is not a single final step, is not confined to Initiation, and does not replace Postfactoring. (SoftwareChange p.5, p.13)

questions.stem: In the phased model, Concept Location sits between which two phases?

options.text: Impact Analysis and Prefactoring
Conclusion and Initiation
Actualization and Postfactoring
Initiation and Impact Analysis

questions.explanation: Concept Location follows Initiation and feeds Impact Analysis: concepts are extracted from the request, located in code, and the located classes seed Impact Analysis. The other pairs are later in the spine. (SoftwareChange p.8)

questions.stem: What does the Impact Analysis phase determine?

options.text: The change strategy and the impact set of affected classes
The natural-language wording of the user story
Which version-control branch to use
The color scheme of the GUI

questions.explanation: Impact Analysis determines the change strategy and the impact set of affected classes. Wording the user story is Initiation; branch choice is Conclusion/version control; GUI styling is unrelated. (SoftwareChange p.9)

questions.stem: How is the initial impact set formed at the start of Impact Analysis?

options.text: From a random sample of supplier modules
From the classes identified during Concept Location
From the classes touched in the previous release
From every class in the entire system

questions.explanation: The classes found in Concept Location make up the initial impact set; dependency analysis then grows it. It is not every class, not last release's classes, and not a random sample. (SoftwareChange p.9)

questions.stem: How does the deck define prefactoring?

options.text: Opportunistic refactoring that localizes (minimizes) the impact of the change
Writing the new feature code
Refactoring done after the change to remove anti-patterns
Committing the change to version control

questions.explanation: Prefactoring is opportunistic refactoring that localizes/minimizes the impact of SC, done before the change. Refactoring after to remove anti-patterns is postfactoring; writing the feature is actualization; committing is conclusion. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: Which two named Fowler refactorings does the deck give as prefactoring examples?

options.text: Rename Variable and Pull Up Method
Extract Class and Extract Superclass
Replace Conditional and Introduce Parameter
Inline Method and Move Field

questions.explanation: The deck names Extract Class (gather fields/methods into a new component) and Extract Superclass (create a new abstract class) as prefactoring examples. The others are real Fowler refactorings but not the ones cited here. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: The Extract Class prefactoring refactoring does what?

options.text: Creates a new abstract superclass
Deletes an unused class
Gathers fields, methods, and code snippets into a new component class
Merges two classes into one

questions.explanation: Extract Class gathers fields, methods, and code snippets into a new component class. Creating a new abstract class is Extract Superclass; deleting and merging are different operations. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: Why is prefactoring performed BEFORE the new code is written?

options.text: To prioritize the change request in the backlog
To remove anti-patterns the change has already introduced
To verify the change against test cases
To make the upcoming change small and local, minimizing its impact

questions.explanation: Prefactoring happens before the change to localize and minimize impact, making the change small and local. Removing introduced anti-patterns is postfactoring (after); verification and backlog prioritization are different phases. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: What does the Actualization phase do?

options.text: Creates new code, plugs it into the old code, and visits neighboring classes to update them
Prioritizes the product backlog
Commits the baseline to version control
Extracts concepts from the change request

questions.explanation: Actualization creates the new code, plugs it into existing code, and updates neighboring classes. Concept extraction is Concept Location, committing is Conclusion, prioritizing is Initiation. (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: During Actualization, what is 'change propagation'?

options.text: Copying the change to every branch
Updating neighboring classes so the system stays consistent
Reverting the change if a test fails
Changes cascading uncontrollably through dependencies

questions.explanation: Change propagation is the deliberate act of updating neighbors to keep the system consistent. The uncontrolled cascade is the ripple effect; the other options are unrelated to the term. (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: How does the 'ripple effect' differ from 'change propagation' in Actualization?

options.text: The ripple effect happens only during Initiation
They are two names for the same act of committing code
The ripple effect is changes cascading outward through dependencies; change propagation is the deliberate updating of neighbors to stay consistent
Change propagation removes anti-patterns introduced by the change

questions.explanation: The ripple effect is the cascade of changes outward through dependencies; change propagation is the maintainer's act of updating neighbors to keep the system consistent. They are distinct, occur in Actualization, and neither is committing or anti-pattern removal. (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: What does the Postfactoring phase eliminate?

options.text: The product backlog
The original change request
Supplier modules from the dependency graph
Anti-patterns that the change may have introduced

questions.explanation: Postfactoring eliminates anti-patterns the change may have introduced (e.g. long method, bloated class). It does not remove the request, suppliers, or the backlog. (SoftwareChange p.12)

questions.stem: Which two anti-patterns does the deck name as targets of postfactoring?

options.text: Long method and bloated class
Feature envy and shotgun surgery
God object and spaghetti code
Magic numbers and dead code

questions.explanation: The deck names long method and bloated class as anti-patterns postfactoring cleans up after a change. The others are real smells but not the cited examples. (SoftwareChange p.12)

questions.stem: Postfactoring is best described as the mirror of which phase?

options.text: Verification, but skipping the tests
Prefactoring, but cleaning up after the change instead of before
Conclusion, but without committing
Actualization, but reverting code instead of adding it

questions.explanation: Postfactoring mirrors prefactoring: prefactoring refactors before to minimize impact, postfactoring refactors after to remove introduced anti-patterns. It is not reverting, skipping tests, or a variant of Conclusion. (SoftwareChange p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: According to the deck, what does Verification comprise?

options.text: Only manual code reading
Only automated unit tests
Testing (functional, unit, structural) and walkthroughs
Compilation and linking

questions.explanation: Verification comprises testing (functional, unit, structural) and walkthroughs. It is broader than manual reading alone, broader than unit tests alone, and is not the build step. (SoftwareChange p.13)

questions.stem: The Conclusion phase is the lecture's explicit tie-in to which later course topics?

options.text: Concept location methodologies
Version Control / Git and continuous integration
User-story templates
Design patterns

questions.explanation: Conclusion (commit, new baseline, prepare for next change) ties into Version Control / Git and CI. Concept location, design patterns, and user stories are introduced in other parts of L02. (SoftwareChange p.14)

questions.stem: According to the deck, what is true about comprehending large programs?

options.text: Comprehension is unnecessary for maintenance
Large programs cannot be completely comprehended
Only the compiler needs to comprehend the program
Large programs must be fully read before any change

questions.explanation: The deck states large programs cannot be completely comprehended, motivating partial comprehension. Reading everything is infeasible, comprehension is needed, and the point concerns the programmer, not the compiler. (ConceptLocation p.3)

questions.stem: What lesson for concept location does the Dog/Pes/Hund example drive home?

options.text: Names in code are always English
Concepts always have exactly one name
The name a programmer searches for may differ from the name used in code, so reason from intension (meaning) to extension (instances)
Extension and intension are the same thing

questions.explanation: Because names vary, the programmer must reason from intension (meaning) to extension (the actual code instances) rather than relying on a literal name match. Concepts can have many names, intension and extension differ, and code names are not always English. (ConceptLocation p.5)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw, which interface represents a drawable element that owns Handles and can itself contain figures?

options.text: DrawApplication
Tool
Figure
Drawing

questions.explanation: Figure is the drawable-element interface; it has 1..n Handles and can itself be a figure container (enabling Composite). Drawing is the top-level container, Tool is the controller, DrawApplication is the window. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: In the MVC class-level picture, which class implements the Tool (controller) interface and exposes mouse methods?

options.text: StandardDrawing, with add()
AbstractFigure, with draw()
FigureAttributes, with an fMap Hashtable
AbstractTool, with mouseDown/Drag/Up/Move and activate/deactivate

questions.explanation: AbstractTool implements the controller interface and exposes mouseDown/Drag/Up/Move plus activate/deactivate. AbstractFigure/StandardDrawing implement model interfaces; FigureAttributes holds attribute state. (JHotDraw p.18)

questions.stem: Which is one of the three underlying GoF design principles cited in the deck?

options.text: Inherit deeply to maximize reuse
Optimize prematurely for performance
Program to an interface, not to an implementation
Avoid all composition

questions.explanation: 'Program to an interface, not to an implementation' is one of the three principles. The others contradict GoF advice (which favors composition over deep inheritance and warns against premature optimization). (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: Which is the third underlying GoF principle in the deck, about variation?

options.text: Find what varies and encapsulate it
Ignore variation entirely
Find what is constant and duplicate it
Find what varies and hard-code it

questions.explanation: The third principle is 'find what varies and encapsulate it'. Duplicating constants, hard-coding variation, and ignoring it are the opposite advice. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: What is the deck's single-sentence thesis linking design patterns to maintainability?

options.text: Extensibility of frameworks calls for extensive use of design patterns
Design patterns slow frameworks down
Frameworks should avoid patterns to stay simple
Patterns matter only for performance

questions.explanation: The thesis is that the extensibility of frameworks calls for extensive use of design patterns. The other statements contradict the deck. (JHotDraw p.16)

questions.stem: Which list correctly assigns three patterns to their categories?

options.text: Singleton structural, Decorator behavioral, Observer creational
Singleton creational, Decorator behavioral, Observer structural
Singleton creational, Decorator structural, Observer behavioral
Singleton behavioral, Decorator creational, Observer structural

questions.explanation: Singleton is creational, Decorator is structural, Observer is behavioral. The other rows scramble at least one assignment. (JHotDraw p.12-14)

questions.stem: Why does a reuse-oriented framework like JHotDraw end up pattern-dense, per the deck's reasoning?

options.text: Because patterns are required by the Java language
Because patterns reduce the number of classes to one
Because frameworks cannot compile without patterns
Because extensibility demands decoupling variation from the fixed skeleton, which is exactly what design patterns provide

questions.explanation: Extensibility requires decoupling what varies from the frozen skeleton, and design patterns are the established way to achieve that, so frameworks become pattern-dense. Patterns are not a language requirement, do not collapse to one class, and are not needed for compilation. (JHotDraw p.9, p.16)

questions.stem: A documented 'con' of Strategy is what?

options.text: Clients must know the strategies, and there is communication overhead and more objects
It forbids algorithm reuse
It removes the ability to choose implementations
It hard-codes the algorithm into the context

questions.explanation: Strategy's cons include that clients must know the strategies, Strategy-Context communication overhead, and more objects. It actually enables algorithm families and implementation choice and decouples (not hard-codes) the algorithm. (JHotDraw p.29)

questions.stem: What is the correct phase order for these activities: (i) commit and build new baseline, (ii) extract Export/Drawing/Text/Format, (iii) determine the impact set, (iv) write 'As a trial user, I want...'?

options.text: (ii), (iv), (iii), (i)
(iv), (iii), (ii), (i)
(iv), (ii), (iii), (i)
(i), (iv), (ii), (iii)

questions.explanation: Writing the user story is Initiation (iv), extracting concepts is Concept Location (ii), determining the impact set is Impact Analysis (iii), and committing/building the baseline is Conclusion (i): order (iv),(ii),(iii),(i). The other orders misplace at least one phase. (SoftwareChange p.5-14)

questions.stem: Why are pre- and post-factoring BOTH considered refactoring under the strict definition?

options.text: Both change structure without changing behavior, only differing in timing relative to the change
Prefactoring changes behavior but postfactoring does not
Both add new behavior to the system
Neither alters code, only documentation

questions.explanation: Refactoring is strictly changing structure without changing behavior; prefactoring (before, to minimize impact) and postfactoring (after, to remove anti-patterns) both satisfy this, differing only in timing. They do not add behavior, both preserve behavior, and both alter code. (SoftwareChange p.4, p.10, p.12, Gotchas)

questions.stem: A student lists the implementation-group phases as 'Postfactoring, Actualization, Prefactoring'. What is wrong?

options.text: Postfactoring is not an implementation phase
Actualization belongs to the design group, not implementation
The order is reversed; it should be Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring
Nothing is wrong; that is the correct order

questions.explanation: The implementation group runs Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring (refactor before, do the change, refactor after). The student reversed it. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: If prefactoring cleans code up before a change goes in, which later phase plays the symmetric clean-up role afterward?

options.text: Initiation
Prefactoring
Concept Location
Impact Analysis

questions.explanation: Prefactoring cleans up before the change to localize impact; postfactoring cleans up after to remove introduced anti-patterns. They mirror each other. (SoftwareChange p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: A new hire asks whether they should read the entire JHotDraw codebase before their first change. What does the deck imply they should expect about comprehending a large program?

options.text: They can always be fully comprehended before any change
They cannot be completely comprehended, so you aim for minimum essential understanding
They require comprehending every class equally
Comprehension is irrelevant to concept location

questions.explanation: Large programs cannot be completely comprehended; the programmer aims for the minimum essential understanding via an as-needed strategy. (ConceptLocation p.3)

questions.stem: Why does the Dog/Pes/Hund example matter for concept location?

options.text: Dogs are the most common domain concept in software
The same concept can have different names, so the name you search for may differ from the name used in code
It proves natural-language names are always identical to code identifiers
It shows extensions are always empty in practice

questions.explanation: 'Dog/Pes/Hund' are different names for one concept; the lesson is that a programmer's search name may differ from the code's name, so reason from intension to extension. (ConceptLocation p.5)

questions.stem: Compare Composite and Decorator as used in JHotDraw: what is the key structural difference the deck draws?

options.text: Composite wraps one component; Decorator aggregates many children
Both aggregate many children identically
Both wrap exactly one component identically
Composite aggregates many children to form a part-whole tree; Decorator wraps exactly one component to add responsibilities

questions.explanation: Composite holds many children (part-whole tree); Decorator wraps a single component and forwards to it while adding behavior. (JHotDraw p.20, p.38)

questions.stem: Starting from the concept-location classes, by what mechanism does Impact Analysis enlarge the impact set?

options.text: All classes in the project are added unconditionally
Class dependencies are analyzed and impacted classes are added to the impact set
Only test classes are added
The set is frozen and never grows

questions.explanation: Impact Analysis analyzes class dependencies and adds impacted classes to the impact set. (SoftwareChange p.9)

questions.stem: One of the four impacts on functionality is defined as reshaping structure while leaving observable behaviour untouched. Which impact is that?

options.text: Refactoring
Incremental
Contraction
Replacement

questions.explanation: Refactoring changes software structure without changing behavior. (SoftwareChange p.4)

questions.stem: What is the point of doing prefactoring ahead of writing any new feature code?

options.text: To make the upcoming change small and local
To verify the change after the fact
To remove anti-patterns the change introduced
To commit the change to version control

questions.explanation: Prefactoring happens before to make the upcoming change small and local. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: A change to one class forces edits in classes that depend on it, which in turn force more edits further out. Which Actualization phenomenon is this?

options.text: Change propagation as a deliberate act
Backtracking
The ripple effect
Prefactoring

questions.explanation: The ripple effect is changes cascading outward through dependencies. (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: Which GoF principle tells you to depend on abstractions rather than concrete classes?

options.text: Favor composition over class inheritance
Find what varies and encapsulate it
Optimize for the common case
Program to an interface, not to an implementation

questions.explanation: 'Program to an interface, not to an implementation' is the principle about depending on abstractions. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: Which GoF principle motivates using object composition instead of deep inheritance hierarchies?

options.text: Favor composition over class inheritance
Program to an interface, not to an implementation
Find what varies and encapsulate it
Keep it simple, stupid

questions.explanation: 'Favor composition over class inheritance' motivates composition over deep inheritance. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: JHotDraw isolates layout algorithms and tool modes behind interfaces so they can vary independently. Which GoF principle does this most directly express?

options.text: Optimize prematurely
Find what varies and encapsulate it
Inherit from concrete classes
Couple modules tightly

questions.explanation: Isolating the varying parts (layouts, modes) behind interfaces expresses 'find what varies and encapsulate it'. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: What does Verification guarantee, and over which phases does it run?

options.text: It guarantees performance and runs only during Initiation
It guarantees security and runs only during Conclusion
It guarantees correctness of the change and runs across the implementation phases
It guarantees nothing and is optional

questions.explanation: Verification guarantees correctness of the change and runs continuously across the implementation phases. (SoftwareChange p.13)

questions.stem: Besides testing, what other activity does the deck list under Verification?

options.text: Profiling
Code minification
Continuous deployment
Walkthroughs

questions.explanation: Verification comprises testing (functional/unit/structural) AND walkthroughs. (SoftwareChange p.13)

questions.stem: Which trio of testing types does the deck explicitly list under Verification?

options.text: Functional, unit, structural
Unit, integration, system
Alpha, beta, gamma
Manual, automated, exploratory

questions.explanation: The deck lists functional, unit, and structural testing under Verification. (SoftwareChange p.13)

questions.stem: In the Conclusion phase, what is committed and built?

options.text: The user story card is committed and a backlog is built
The finished code is committed to version control and a new baseline is built
The impact set is committed and a CDG is built
The test report is committed and a prototype is built

questions.explanation: Conclusion commits the finished code to version control and builds the new baseline. (SoftwareChange p.14)

questions.stem: After forking JHotDraw on GitHub for the ChangeReqLab, which collaboration workflow do students adopt?

options.text: Trunk-based with no branches
GitFlow with release trains
GitHub flow
Subversion centralized commits

questions.explanation: The ChangeReqLab has students fork JHotDraw and adopt GitHub flow. (ChangeReqLab p.1)

questions.stem: The Conclusion phase explicitly ties the change process to which later course themes?

options.text: Version control and continuous integration
Design patterns and OO principles
Concept triangle and intension
BDD scenarios and Gherkin

questions.explanation: Conclusion (commit, new baseline) ties into Version Control / Git and CI. (SoftwareChange p.14)

questions.stem: Which Lientz & Swanson category most directly maps to reducing technical debt by improving structure before problems arise?

options.text: Corrective
Preventive
Adaptive
Perfective

questions.explanation: Preventive maintenance improves code structure to forestall future problems, closely related to refactoring and reducing technical debt. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: Postfactoring removes a 'bloated class' a change introduced. In technical-debt terms, what is postfactoring doing?

options.text: Adding new debt deliberately
Deferring all cleanup to the next release
Paying down debt the change just incurred by removing the introduced anti-pattern
Committing the bloated class as the new baseline

questions.explanation: Postfactoring eliminates anti-patterns (e.g. bloated class) the change introduced — paying down the freshly incurred debt. (SoftwareChange p.12)
